caveats: a: O*NET occupations span the whole economy and are not the observed occupation mix of 512120.

a: Rights and royalty data are structured in some firms but remain contract-specific and exception-heavy in others.

rho: Cineverse is explicitly technology-first and may not represent typical lower-middle-market distributors.
rho: Platform delivery and rights errors can create disproportionate financial and reputational losses.

e: Rights-holding entities and operating distributors may be counted separately or under common ownership.
e: The supplied firm estimate is margin-bridged despite title volatility, minimum guarantees, and capitalized content costs.

s5: Catalog, rights, platform, and media-service transactions may fall outside 512120 or fail the control test.

d5: A few successful or unsuccessful titles can dominate annual company comparisons.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Distributors earn recurring or repeat distribution commissions, agency fees, platform and media-service fees, fixed license fees, minimum guarantees with overages, and shares of box-office, rental, advertising, subscription, or other title receipts across territories and release windows.
lens.excluded_activities: Companies primarily producing and distributing their own content classified in 512110; captive distribution units; title-only rights vehicles and passive libraries without an operating team; physical-media wholesalers; exhibitors; streaming infrastructure providers; shells; non-control financing vehicles; and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market distributors that repeatedly acquire, license, represent, market, deliver, monetize, report, and settle rights to motion pictures and video with theaters, broadcasters, networks, streaming services, digital platforms, and other exhibitors or licensees.

a.bridge: O*NET's cross-industry market-research and marketing-management tasks and public distributor workflows are mapped to the wage-weighted work of 512120 firms. Title ingestion, metadata, availability matching, audience analysis, demand forecasting, release planning, asset search, localization, marketing variants, delivery validation, usage reconciliation, royalty reporting, and contract abstraction are treated as exposed; rights judgment, negotiations, relationships, financing, final campaign accountability, and exception resolution remain less exposed. The occupational sources are not 512120-specific and emphasize computer-based analytical work, so the mapping likely overstates exposure where senior sales and rights relationships dominate payroll.
caveats: O*NET occupations span the whole economy and are not the observed occupation mix of 512120.
Integrated studios and technology-led distributors may have different staffing from independent sales agents.
Rights and royalty data are structured in some firms but remain contract-specific and exception-heavy in others.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted activity study across independent theatrical, television, international-sales, and digital distributors, separating current automation from rights acquisition, sales, marketing, localization, delivery, collections, royalty accounting, legal, and management tasks.
a.rationale: Modern distribution is largely an information, rights, and digital-supply-chain business, creating substantial exposure in repeat data handling and analysis. High-value work still depends on title selection, territorial knowledge, buyer trust, negotiation, and accountable rights decisions.

d5.bridge: Lionsgate's fiscal-2026 Motion Picture revenue rose 1.2%, with digital home-entertainment and international revenue increasing while television and packaged media declined; Cineverse's fiscal-2026 revenue fell 16% against a hit-title comparison while quarterly viewers and minutes streamed rose by more than 50%. These volatile company and channel measures are converted into a roughly flat five-year US constant-quality distribution-service quantity. Hit slates and nominal pricing can bias revenue upward, while shifting activity to automated and direct digital channels can raise viewing without proportionate distributor service volume.
caveats: Both companies combine production, distribution, streaming, libraries, technology, and other media services.
Nominal title revenue and audience minutes are not constant-price, constant-quality distributor service quantity.
A few successful or unsuccessful titles can dominate annual company comparisons.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year series of independently distributed titles, active rights contracts, platform and territorial deliveries, licensed viewing hours, release windows, inflation-adjusted distributor fees, and producer self-distribution rates, segmented by theatrical, television, streaming, FAST, and international markets.
d5.rationale: Audiences, platforms, and exhibitors continue to demand titles across more windows and digital outlets, and catalog monetization adds repeat activity. Linear and physical decline, platform consolidation, direct licensing, hit concentration, and fewer release intermediaries offset growth in streaming, advertising-supported channels, and international digital delivery.

caveats: Industry classification can shift integrated producer-distributors into 512110 even when distribution is operationally substantial.
Rights-holding entities and operating distributors may be counted separately or under common ownership.
The supplied firm estimate is margin-bridged despite title volatility, minimum guarantees, and capitalized content costs.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 512120 census linking establishments to common ownership, normalized EBITDA, active catalogs, third-party titles, recurring counterparties, contract transferability, key-person dependence, territorial rights, and service versus principal-at-risk revenue.
e.rationale: Independent distributors with recurring filmmaker, studio, platform, and exhibitor relationships can fit the lens and retain transferable teams, contracts, and systems. Eligibility is reduced because integrated producer-distributors belong in 512110, some entities hold only title rights, customer and key-person concentration is high, and the supplied margin bridge may misclassify volatile slate economics.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 512120 ($1,232,947,036) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 512120 ($2,080,500,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1728 (margins.json[51]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Information Services EBITDA/Sales 17.28% (conservative sector default)); revenue/employee $739,602 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 512120); class <5: 348 firms, avg revenue/firm $708,810 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $122,482 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,380,474 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $411,346 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,914,941 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,194,902 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 26 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,692,308 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,057,231 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $172,091,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,737,359 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 60

caveats: Operator requirement differs sharply between theatrical and international releases and simple digital catalog delivery.
A platform, producer, or technology vendor can internalize the same accountable functions.
AI-generated or self-served content may expand title volume while reducing the value of traditional intermediation.
o.change_evidence: Cohorts of independent producers and libraries showing direct-to-platform adoption, distributor replacement, fee compression, delivery and rights failures, time to collection, territory coverage, and the share of titles commercially exploited without an external distributor or sales agent.
o.rationale: Rights owners can increasingly license directly to platforms or adopt automated supply-chain tools, and digital delivery removes much physical intermediation. Many independent titles still need an accountable distributor or sales agent to finance minimum guarantees, clear and aggregate rights, select windows and territories, pitch buyers, manage theatrical and platform releases, validate delivery, collect receipts, reconcile royalties, and resolve exceptions.

q.bridge: Lionsgate's picture-by-picture box-office participation, digital revenue sharing, commissions, fixed fees, minimum guarantees, overages, territory and window licenses, and cost recoupment are translated into the discounted benefit retained by lower-middle-market distributors after renewal and sharing. Lionsgate owns major content and has scale and leverage beyond a pure independent distributor, so its structures likely bias retained benefit upward even though they demonstrate the relevant contract mechanisms.
caveats: Integrated-studio agreements may not represent independent third-party distribution contracts.
Revenue share and minimum guarantees describe payment mechanics, not direct pass-through of automation savings.
Rights owners can shift distributors or self-distribute when service becomes standardized.
q.change_evidence: Representative distribution agreements and renewal cohorts showing fee bases, expense caps, minimum guarantees, overage formulas, recoupment waterfalls, audit rights, platform deductions, technology charges, fee resets, and distributor margin before and after automation.
q.rationale: Percentage fees and commissions can preserve workflow savings because compensation is linked to receipts rather than internal labor cost, while fixed platform or service fees also allow temporary margin capture. Producers and rights owners can demand lower fees at renewal, platforms exert bargaining power, transparent expenses are recouped, and title underperformance or service failures remain with the distributor.

rho.bridge: Adobe demonstrates available media search and caption-localization capabilities, Lionsgate reports beginning to incorporate AI-enabled tools, and Cineverse reports an automated content supply chain, AI metadata, ad optimization, and planned AI-enabled cost reduction. These large or technology-forward operators are translated into five-year implementation at lower-middle-market distributors; their proprietary systems, scale, and engineering resources likely bias the base upward for ordinary independents.
caveats: Management descriptions of AI strategy are not controlled measures of realized labor removal.
Cineverse is explicitly technology-first and may not represent typical lower-middle-market distributors.
Platform delivery and rights errors can create disproportionate financial and reputational losses.
rho.change_evidence: Operational telemetry from content-supply-chain, rights-management, localization, ad-tech, and royalty platforms showing customer-size adoption, straight-through processing, exception and override rates, delivery failures, labor hours removed, implementation cost, and client retention.
rho.rationale: Digital assets, repeated platform specifications, large title catalogs, standardized metadata, and measurable campaign and viewing data support faster implementation than in physical production. Fragmented rights, legacy contracts, platform certification, cybersecurity, auditability, client approvals, and small technical teams constrain rollout.

caveats: Two acquisitions by one technology-led company do not measure the industry transfer probability.
Catalog, rights, platform, and media-service transactions may fall outside 512120 or fail the control test.
No owner-age or succession distribution is observed for eligible independent distributors.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of entertainment transactions from SEC filings, specialist investment banks, M&A databases, distributor associations, and seller interviews, matched to eligible 512120 firms and coded for control, operating-team continuity, title-only versus enterprise scope, seller ownership, and normalized EBITDA.
s5.rationale: Strategic acquisition demand exists for distribution platforms, media-service providers, ad technology, catalogs, and customer relationships; Cineverse completed two adjacent acquisitions in early 2026. That evidence does not provide a representative transfer denominator, and many deals acquire titles, minority interests, or technology rather than control of an eligible 512120 operating firm.

principal_driver: Digital catalogs, repeated release workflows, structured platform data, and measurable audience behavior make distribution operations unusually amenable to automation.
principal_weakness: The same digitalization lowers intermediation barriers and lets producers, platforms, and technology vendors absorb the distributor's role.
